ontology.foodon_gap_note: FOODON_00002473 covers bento as a food product category; no exact code for beef-and-teriyaki-chicken bento exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers rice-based mixed dishes; no dedicated code for beef and teriyaki chicken bento. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bento box serving is approximately 350 g, comprising a portion of steamed white rice alongside stir-fried beef slices and teriyaki-glazed chicken with accompanying vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Beef and Teriyaki Chicken Bento — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Beef and Teriyaki Chicken Bento (cr_id F05100004). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice and mixed rice-based dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef and teriyaki chicken bento. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate component being white jasmine/short-grain rice, which has a well-established GI of approximately 64–72 in the University of Sydney GI Database. The teriyaki sauce and protein components modestly attenuate the glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 19.5 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 22.2 g per 350 g bento box serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 310 mg/100 g from teriyaki and soy sauce base
hypertension.guidance: At 310 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1,085 mg per 350 g serving), this bento provides over 45% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit in a single meal; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium teriyaki sauce or sauce on the side and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice dominates carbohydrate fraction (19.5 g/100 g, GI ~65, low fibre-to-carb ratio)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice base contributes a medium-to-high glycaemic load (~22 g GL per serving) with only 2.2 g dietary fibre per 100 g; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request a brown rice or cauliflower rice substitution and ask for reduced-sugar teriyaki sauce to blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and saturated fat from beef and teriyaki sauce; moderate total fat 4.27 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of 310 mg/100 g sodium and saturated fat from beef raises cardiovascular risk; individuals with CVD should limit portion size, choose the chicken-only option to reduce saturated fat, and request low-sodium sauce to keep the meal within heart-healthy dietary targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef provides arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies RA inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Red meat in this bento supplies arachidonic acid that can amplify inflammatory pathways in rheumatoid arthritis; individuals with RA should request a chicken-only bento, retain the vegetable components (broccoli, edamame) for their anti-inflammatory sulforaphane and isoflavone content, and ask for reduced-sodium teriyaki sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef and Teriyaki Chicken Bento is a Japanese-style bento box meal popular in Singapore's food courts and Japanese casual-dining outlets. It consists of steamed white rice accompanied by stir-fried sliced beef and teriyaki-glazed chicken pieces, typically served alongside steamed or stir-fried vegetables such as broccoli, edamame, and carrots. The teriyaki sauce — a sweet-savoury glaze made from soy sauce, mirin, and sugar — imparts a characteristic umami-rich, slightly caramelised flavour to both proteins. The rice is soft and mildly sticky, the beef is tender and savoury, and the chicken is juicy with a glossy teriyaki coating. Nutritionally, the dish provides 152 kcal per 100 g, with 8.8 g protein, 4.27 g fat, and 19.5 g carbohydrate. Sodium is elevated at 310 mg/100 g due to the soy-sauce-based teriyaki glaze, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The white rice base carries a medium glycaemic index (~65), and the low dietary fibre content (2.2 g/100 g) means blood glucose management requires attention for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The beef component contains arachidonic acid, which is a concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is tagged halal and is a common working-lunch option in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100004) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and canonical. GI is an analogous estimate based on the white rice component using the University of Sydney GI Database, which introduces minor uncertainty but does not affect the overall high confidence in the nutritional profile.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g (1.4 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite data for mixed beef-and-chicken rice dishes, as the HPB source does not report saturated fat separately; this value should be verified against a direct HPB or ICMR-NIN measurement when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because the HPB Foodo source does not report these values for this specific composite dish entry, and no secondary approved source provides a sufficiently close match to substitute these values with confidence. 3. GI value of 65 is an analogous estimate based on the white rice dominant carbohydrate fraction using University of Sydney GI Database reference data for white rice (GI 64–72); no direct GI measurement for this specific bento product was found in approved sources. 4. The dish is tagged halal per the input record; this reflects the Singapore food-court preparation context. 5. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for beef-and-teriyaki-chicken bento as a named composite dish.
